result.markdown: --- title: "[logrank_power()]{.doc-object-name .doc-function .doc-label .doc-label-function}" body-classes: doc-api-page page-navigation: false html-table-processing: none --- ::: {.doc-subject} Power of the log-rank test given the number of observed events. ::: ::: {.doc-usage-source} Usage [Source](https://github.com/rich-iannone/greenwood/blob/main/greenwood/_power.py#L183-L294){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} ::: ::: {.doc-signature .doc-Kind.FUNCTION} ```python logrank_power(hazard_ratio, n_events, *, alpha=0.05, allocation=0.5, sides=2) ``` ::: ::: {.doc-text} Computes the statistical power to detect a specified hazard ratio using the log-rank test, given a fixed number of observed events in a two-group survival study. This is the inverse calculation of `logrank_n_events`: instead of finding the events needed for a target power, this function finds the power achieved with a given number of events. Power depends on three factors: 1. **Number of events** (`n_events`): More events → higher power 2. **Effect size** (`hazard_ratio`): Larger effects (HR far from 1.0) → higher power 3. **Significance level** (`alpha`): More stringent (smaller alpha) → lower power Under the proportional hazards assumption, power depends only on the total event count, not the follow-up duration, censoring rate, or sample size separately. This makes it a practical tool for updating power calculations as events accumulate during a trial. ::: ## Parameters {.doc-parameters} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [hazard_ratio]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : The hazard ratio to detect (group 2 versus group 1). Can be < 1 (group 2 has lower hazard/better survival) or > 1 (group 2 has higher hazard/worse survival). The result is symmetric: HR=0.5 and HR=2.0 give the same power. [n_events]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Total number of observed events. Must be positive. Power increases with more events; even small trials can have high power if many events occur. [alpha]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[0.05]{.fl}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Significance level (Type-I error rate, default 0.05). The probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it's true. Use `alpha=0.05` for two-sided tests with p < 0.05 threshold. [allocation]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[0.5]{.fl}]{.doc-parameter-default} : Fraction of subjects in one group (default 0.5, a balanced design). For unbalanced designs (e.g., 0.3, 0.7), power decreases; balanced allocation minimizes the total sample size needed for a target power. [sides]{.doc-parameter-name}[:]{.doc-parameter-annotation-sep} [int]{.doc-parameter-annotation} [=]{.doc-parameter-default-sep .op} [[2]{.dv}]{.doc-parameter-default} : 1 (one-sided) or 2 (two-sided, default). One-sided tests have higher power but test directional hypotheses only. Two-sided tests are standard but require more events for the same power. ::: ## Returns {.doc-returns} ::: {.doc-definition-items} [float]{.doc-parameter-annotation} : Statistical power, a value between 0 and 1. Power of 0.8 (80%) is conventional in many fields; higher power (0.9, 0.95) requires more events. ::: ## Details {.doc-details} **Schoenfeld's formula**: Under proportional hazards, the power of the log-rank test is $$ \mathrm{Power} = \Phi\!\left(\sqrt{d \, p \, (1-p)} \; |\ln(\mathrm{HR})| - z_{1-\alpha/\mathrm{sides}}\right), $$ where $d$ is the number of events, $p$ is the allocation fraction, and $\Phi$ is the cumulative normal distribution function. This formula is exact under proportional hazards and asymptotically valid for finite samples. **Practical use**: During a running trial, as events accumulate, you can use this function to assess interim power. If interim power is low despite many events, the effect size may be smaller than anticipated. ## Examples {.doc-examples} A study expects to observe 60 events over its follow-up period. What power does it have to detect a hazard ratio of 0.5 (50% hazard reduction)? ::: {#4680bb13 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import greenwood as gw gw.logrank_power(hazard_ratio=0.5, n_events=60) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ``` 0.7656462084964221 ``` ::: ::: This power (~0.9) is typical for a well-designed trial. Lower power suggests more events are needed, or the effect size is smaller than assumed. Compute power for different effect sizes to understand study sensitivity: ::: {#742b94bc .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} for hr in [0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8]: power = gw.logrank_power(hazard_ratio=hr, n_events=60) print(f"HR {hr}: power = {power:.2%}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` HR 0.5: power = 76.56% HR 0.6: power = 50.74% HR 0.7: power = 28.14% HR 0.8: power = 13.66% ``` ::: ::: Use `sides=1` for a one-sided test (higher power, but assumes direction is known): ::: {#33548dec .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} gw.logrank_power(hazard_ratio=0.5, n_events=60, sides=1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` 0.8507589229028263 ``` ::: ::: Use unbalanced allocation if one group is larger. Power decreases with imbalance: ::: {#c3346a06 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} gw.logrank_power(hazard_ratio=0.5, n_events=60, allocation=0.3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` 0.691625951433357 ``` ::: :::
